note_html:
Subjective:
HPI: The patient, William, presents with a right knee injury sustained while trampolining a week ago, which he describes as a "snap" with instant pain. He has been experiencing pain on the outside of the knee, with no improvement with Tylenol. He has tried elevating the knee, but that is the extent of his self-care. He denies any other symptoms.

Past medical history: The patient has hypertension, for which he is currently taking 20mg of lisinopril. He also has diabetes and is taking metformin 500mg. He has not been consistent with his exercise regimen and has a diet that could be improved.

Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]

Current medications:
Lisinopril 20mg daily for hypertension
Metformin 500mg daily for diabetes

Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 180/80, which is above the desired range.

Physical exam: On examination, the patient has a slight gait and a limp. The knee has ecchymosis and edema on the lateral aspect, but no effusion. Range of motion is decent, but the patient experiences pain with movement.

Test Results: The right knee x-ray shows no evidence of fracture or bony abnormality.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with a lateral ligament strain of the right knee and hypertension that is not well-controlled.

Problem list: Right knee pain; lateral ligament strain; hypertension; diabetes.

Plan:
I prescribed meloxicam 15mg daily for pain and swelling related to the lateral ligament strain. I referred the patient to physical therapy to help strengthen the muscles around the area and prevent further injury. I continued the lisinopril at 20mg daily for hypertension and ordered an echocardiogram to assess cardiac function. I also ordered an A1c and lipid panel to evaluate the patient's diabetes management.

Follow-up: The patient will return for a follow-up appointment to discuss the results of the tests and to reassess the plan.
